title: Genesis 16
subtitle: Hagar and Ishmael; the God Who Sees

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — Sarai's Plan; Hagar and Abram

notes.note: MacArthur: Sarai's proposal to give her maidservant to Abram follows the accepted custom of the ancient world for barren wives. What is culturally conventional is not necessarily divinely approved. The plan is a human solution to a divine promise -- attempting to fulfil God's word through human means, the pattern that produces Ishmael rather than Isaac.

MacArthur: Abraham's response -- 'do with her whatever you think best' -- abdicates responsibility. He is the head of the household and the covenant recipient; the welfare of both Sarai and Hagar is his charge. Passive abdication of responsibility in a domestic conflict is the patriarch's failure here, not Sarai's harshness alone.

heb.paragraph: Hagar's social status as šiplāh (v.1) places her at the lowest tier of the household — not merely a servant but a piece of property that Sarai legally owns and can dispose of. The institution Sarai invokes — giving a slave to the husband as a surrogate — is well-documented in ancient Near Eastern law codes (Code of Hammurabi §§144-145; Nuzi tablets). The practice is legal; the theology is complex: Sarai is taking a legal instrument of culture to achieve what only a divine promise can properly provide.

"Perhaps (ʾûlay) I can build a family through her" (v.2) — Sarai's ʾûlay is the language of human engineering attempting to work around divine timing. The same uncertainty-word appears in the Sodom intercession (18:24, 28-32). Here it marks the gap between divine promise and human experience of delay — Sarai is ten years into waiting (v.3) and the ʾûlay of faith has shifted to a ʾûlay of human strategy. The narrative presents this with compassion, not condemnation, but the consequences unfold accordingly.

heb.gloss: to be despised / to treat lightly / to curse
heb.paragraph: Once Hagar conceived, "she began to despise (watteqal) her mistress" (v.4). The verb qālal (the root of the noun "curse") is used of treating something as of no weight or worth — the opposite of kābēd (to honour/give weight). The same root appears in the Noahic curse on Canaan (9:25). Hagar's contempt for Sarai reverses the social order — the šiplāh now looks down on her owner — and triggers the conflict that will run through the rest of the chapter.

hist.historical: The practice of using a servant as a surrogate mother for a barren wife is well-documented in ANE law codes. The Nuzi tablets (15th c. BC) contain a marriage contract stipulating that if a wife is barren, she must provide her husband with a servant-wife. The Code of Hammurabi also addresses the legal status of children born to servant-wives. Sarai's suggestion, though morally problematic from a later biblical perspective, was standard ANE legal procedure.
hist.context: Sarai's plan is a "help" that creates catastrophic complications — a human attempt to fulfill the divine promise through human wisdom. The parallel with Eden (3:6) is strong: both involve a human shortcut that bypasses trust in God. Yet Genesis does not simply condemn Sarai — her anguish is understandable, the cultural context makes her solution reasonable. The narrative is compassionate to all parties while showing the cost of bypassing divine timing.

refs.note: The Hagar-Ishmael story has two episodes: this initial flight (ch. 16) and the later expulsion (ch. 21). Together they form a diptych exploring the tensions of surrogate motherhood, divine promise, and human impatience.

Paul allegorises Hagar and Sarah as two covenants: Sinai (slavery) and the Jerusalem above (freedom). However complex Paul's reading, it confirms the theological weight the early church placed on this narrative.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarai's proposal — that Abram take her Egyptian slave Hagar as a surrogate — was legally normative in the ancient Near East. Sarna: "Several ancient Near Eastern legal codes (Hammurabi, Nuzi tablets) provide for exactly this arrangement: a barren wife could give her slave to her husband, and any child born would be considered the wife's child. Sarai is not acting immorally by the standards of her world — she is following established legal practice."

Sarai's accusation — "you are responsible for the wrong I am suffering" — begins a pattern of blame that will recur in the Abraham story. Sarna: "Sarai holds Abram responsible for the consequences of her own plan. Abram's response — 'your slave is in your hands' — withdraws from responsibility entirely. Both responses reveal the relational damage created by the surrogate arrangement."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The irony of "after Abram had been living in Canaan ten years" is not accidental. Alter: "Ten years have passed since the promise. The divine word has not been fulfilled. Sarai's plan is the human solution to the divine delay — taking the promise into her own hands rather than waiting for God's timing. The narrative presents the plan without endorsing it; the consequences will show its inadequacy."

Vate'anneha Sarai — "and Sarai mistreated her." The verb anah (afflict, humble) is the same word used for Israel's oppression in Egypt (Exod 1:11-12). Alter: "The Egyptian slave Hagar is afflicted by the matriarch Sarah — and she flees to Egypt via the desert. The narrative quietly echoes the Exodus pattern: affliction, flight, divine rescue. Hagar's story is a mini-Exodus, and she becomes the first person in the Bible to receive a divine promise for her child."

Sarai's plan reflects the natural human tendency to help God keep his promises. Calvin: "We perceive how greatly the faith of Sarai was shaken, so that instead of depending on God, she laid hold of an unlawful remedy. Thus unbelief is always in a hurry, and cannot wait for God." The surrogate plan is not an act of bold faith but of impatient unbelief — an attempt to provide for God what God has not yet provided.

Calvin on the blame exchange: "We see how great is the perversity of human nature. Sarai could not endure that her maid should be treated with more consideration than herself. And Abram, in order to escape contention, gave up his wife to her caprice." The domestic conflict reveals the fruit of departing from God's plan — the household is damaged by the plan Sarai initiated.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the surrogate arrangement confirms the legal background from ancient Near Eastern sources. The note cites the Nuzi tablets specifically, which document similar practices. The note observes that the legal normalcy of the practice does not mean it was God's plan — the narrative will show the arrangement creating conflict that persists through the entire Abraham story and beyond.

The NET note on Hagar's contempt discusses the social dynamics: in ancient Near Eastern surrogate arrangements, bearing a child elevated the surrogate's status, which could destabilise the household hierarchy. Hagar's contempt is the natural result of a custom that elevated the child-bearer.

sections.header: Verses 7–16 — The Angel Finds Hagar; Ishmael Promised

poi.name: Shur road / Beer Lahai Roi
poi.text: The road to Shur connects Canaan to Egypt — Hagar is fleeing toward her homeland. The angel finds her at a spring on this road. Beer Lahai Roi ("well of the Living One who sees me") becomes a named covenant location: Hagar's encounter gives a place its theological name.

poi.name: Beer Lahai Roi
poi.coords: Between Kadesh and Bered in the Negev
poi.text: This well is mentioned again in 24:62 and 25:11 as Isaac's dwelling place. The son of the promise lives at the well named by the Egyptian slave-woman's encounter with God. The geography of covenant grace has no simple boundaries.

notes.note: MacArthur: The Angel of the LORD finds Hagar at a spring in the wilderness -- the first of many divine encounters in the wilderness that anticipate Israel's wilderness experience. The Angel's questions ('Where have you come from, and where are you going?') are not requests for information but pastoral interventions calling Hagar to self-awareness.

MacArthur: Hagar named God El Roi -- 'the God who sees me' -- the only place in Scripture where a human gives God a new name. A foreign slave woman, fleeing and alone, becomes the recipient of divine encounter. God's covenant care extends beyond the covenant family to all who encounter him in their need.

heb.paragraph: The "angel of the LORD" (v.7) appears for the first time in Scripture — a figure who speaks as God in the first person (v.10: "I will increase your descendants so much that they will be too numerous to count"), yet is distinguishable from God. The malʾak YHWH ("messenger/angel of the LORD") is the OT's mediatorial presence — God making himself accessible in human encounter. The same figure guides Hagar, calls Abraham at the Akedah (22:11), and appears to Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3:2).

The angel names the child Ishmael (yišmāʿ-ʾēl — "God will hear/God hears") because "the LORD has heard of your misery" (v.11). The name encodes the divine response to Hagar's suffering: her cry has been heard. The name is simultaneously a theological statement (God hears the cry of the afflicted) and a covenant marker (the Ishmaelite tradition preserves the memory of divine attentiveness to a slave woman in the wilderness). Ishmael's descendants carry his name's testimony forward.

heb.gloss: Well of the Living One Who Sees Me
heb.paragraph: Hagar names the spring "Beer Lahai Roi" — "the well of the Living One who sees me" (v.14), asking: "Have I truly seen the One who sees me?" The divine title El Roi ("God who sees me," v.13) is one of the OT's most intimate — used by a foreign slave woman who has been used, abandoned, and found by God in the wilderness. Her theology of divine seeing anticipates Psalm 139's "you perceive my thoughts from afar... you are familiar with all my ways."

hist.context: The Hagar theophany is one of the Bible's most moving passages. A pregnant runaway slave encounters the Angel of the LORD, who calls her by name, acknowledges her pain, and gives her son a promise rivaling Abraham's own. She is the first person in Scripture to receive a divine messenger, the first to give God a name, and the first to receive a birth announcement. None of this is incidental — Genesis is making a theological statement about divine care for the marginalized.

refs.note: In ch.21 Hagar is again in the wilderness, again heard by God, again given a promise for Ishmael. The pattern repeats: human rejection, divine encounter, divine provision.

Paul uses Hagar and Sarah allegorically: Hagar represents the Sinai covenant (law-based slavery), Sarah the new covenant (promise-based freedom). The allegory has been controversial in its effect on Hagar's legacy.

The birth announcement to Hagar ("you shall call his name…") uses exactly the same formula as Gabriel's announcement to Mary: "You shall call his name Jesus." Hagar's encounter is a prototype of the annunciation.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The angel of the LORD finds Hagar at a spring in the wilderness — the first theophany to a woman in the Bible, and to an Egyptian slave-woman at that. Sarna: "Hagar's encounter with the divine is the most remarkable in the Abraham narrative for its recipients: she is female, a foreigner, a slave, and at the lowest point of her life. God's care reaches precisely those whom the social hierarchy has rendered invisible."

Hagar names God: El Roi — "the God who sees me." Sarna: "She is the only person in the Bible who names God. The name expresses her astonishment: 'Have I really seen God and remained alive after seeing him?' The God who is visible to the powerful (Abraham, who receives elaborate covenant ceremonies) is also visible to the powerless slave-woman in the wilderness."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The angel's questions to Hagar — "where have you come from, and where are you going?" — echo God's questions in the garden narrative (3:9) but with different weight. Alter: "Hagar is not interrogated as a transgressor but as a lost person. 'Where have you come from, and where are you going?' — both questions are open: she has fled from affliction, and her destination is unknown. The angel meets her in the in-between space of her flight."

Hagar's naming of God is extraordinary. Alter: "In the patriarchal narratives, it is always God who renames — Abram to Abraham, Jacob to Israel. Here, a slave woman names God. The reversal is deliberate: the one who has no status names the one who has all status. El Roi — the God who sees — receives his name from the one no one else sees."

The angel commands Hagar to return — to submit to Sarai. Calvin notes the severity of this: "God does not here approve of the cruelty of Sarai; but he teaches Hagar that she must not seek a remedy for her evils by unlawful means. She is commanded to return, not because her affliction was just, but because the due order of the household could not be maintained in any other way."

The naming of God by Hagar shows the universality of divine concern. Calvin: "God does not confine his care to the elect only. The fact that the angel appeared to Hagar, who was not among the elect, demonstrates that God's providence extends over all creatures, even those outside the covenant. He who sees all things was moved to mercy by the distress of this foreign slave-woman."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "the angel of the LORD" discusses the long-debated question of whether the angel is a separate being or a manifestation of God himself. The note observes that in this and other passages, the angel speaks in the first person as God (v.10: "I will increase your descendants so much..."), is addressed as God by Hagar (v.13), and yet is distinguished from God. The note identifies this as a theophanic angel — a manifestation of the divine presence in angelomorphic form.

The NET note on Ishmael ("God hears") observes that the name is given before the birth — a divine naming, not a parental one. The promise about Ishmael's character — "a wild donkey of a man; his hand will be against everyone and everyone's hand against him" — is discussed as a characterisation of the Bedouin lifestyle of his descendants, not a moral condemnation.

ppl.name: Hagar
ppl.text: Receives the most personal divine encounter in the patriarchal narratives — addressed by name, asked about her journey, given a son's name and a promise. First person in the Bible to receive a birth announcement, first to name God.

ppl.name: Sarai (Sarah)
ppl.role: Wife of Abram; initiates the surrogate plan
ppl.text: Her solution is culturally reasonable and humanly understandable — but bypasses trust in God's timing. The text is sympathetic to her anguish while showing the cost of the decision.

ppl.name: Ishmael
ppl.role: Son of Abraham and Hagar; ancestor of Arab peoples
ppl.text: Given a promise of nationhood parallel to Isaac's, though distinct. Genesis 17:20 reaffirms God's blessing on Ishmael — he is not abandoned, only repositioned in the covenant narrative.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"She gave this name to the LORD who spoke to her: 'You are the God who sees me,' for she said, 'I have now seen the One who sees me.'"ESV"So she called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, 'You are a God of seeing,' for she said, 'Truly here I have seen him who looks after me.'"KJV"And she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me: for she said, Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?"NASB"Then she called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, 'You are a God who sees'; for she said, 'Have I even remained alive here after seeing Him?'"

src.title: Nuzi Marriage Contract (HSS V, 67)
src.quote: A contract stipulating: "If Gilimninnu [the wife] does not bear children, Gilimninnu shall take a woman of N/Lullu-land [i.e., a slave woman] as a wife for Shennima [the husband]" — the precise legal arrangement of Gen 16.
src.note: The parallel shows Sarai's action was legally normative in the patriarchal world, not a moral failure in its cultural context.

rec.text: Trible includes Hagar among the "texts of terror" — women who suffer in the Bible. She reads the text as exposing the violence of patriarchal structures against the marginalized.

rec.who: Delores Williams, Sisters in the Wilderness (1993)
rec.text: Womanist theologian Williams reads Hagar as the paradigmatic figure for Black women in America — suffering, dismissed, yet encountered by God in the wilderness. Her reading has been enormously influential in liberationist theology.

Calvin emphasized the Angel of the LORD as a Christophany and Hagar's name for God (El Roi) as evidence that true knowledge of God was not limited to Israel but available to those outside the covenant community when God chose to reveal himself.

rows.label: Hagar's Encounter: The God Who Sees
rows.text: Hagar names God El Roi -- the God who sees me. A foreign slave becomes the first person in Scripture to give God a personal name. No one is outside God's sight, not even the covenant's margin.

rows.label: Ishmael Promised: Grace Beyond the Covenant Line
rows.text: The angel's promise to Hagar uses the covenant multiplication formula. God gives to the covenant's outside what he gives to the inside -- the blessing's scope exceeds its immediate vehicle.

hebtext.note: Hagar's social status as šiplāh (v.1) places her at the lowest tier of the household — not merely a servant but a piece of property that Sarai legally owns and can dispose of. The institution Sarai invokes — giving a slave to the husband as a surrogate — is well-documented in ancient Near Eastern law codes (Code of Hammurabi §§144-145; Nuzi tablets). tx.title: Sarai's complaint — 'May the LORD judge'
tx.content: MT reads 'May the LORD judge between me and you' (yišpōṭ YHWH). LXX translates with κρίναι (judge/decide). Targum Onqelos paraphrases: 'May the LORD make manifest the wrong between me and you,' softening the legal language into a plea for vindication rather than judicial condemnation.
tx.note: The Targum's adjustment reveals discomfort with depicting the matriarch in heated legal dispute with the patriarch — a tendency visible across the Targumim.

debate.title: Hagar and Ishmael: The Status of the Rejected Line

positions.name: Outside the covenant
positions.argument: The Abrahamic covenant runs through Isaac (17:21; 21:12). Ishmael is blessed (17:20) but is not the covenant heir. Paul's allegory (Gal 4:21-31) uses Hagar/Ishmael as the figure of the law-covenant vs. the promise-covenant.

positions.name: Within divine care
positions.argument: The Genesis narrative gives Hagar and Ishmael extensive divine attention: two angelic appearances (16:7-12; 21:17-19), a personal divine name (El Roi), a promise for Ishmael (17:20; 21:18), and God hearing his cry (21:17). The rejected line is not abandoned.
